comments.text: I would like to mention a related preprint: Xinjun Wang, “A $667/806$ Upper Bound for Erdős Problem #301 on Unit-Fraction-Free Sets”, May 2026. It says $ f(N) \leq (667/806+o(1))N $, where $667/806 \approx 0.827543$. This improves the upper bound currently recorded on this page, $ f(N) \leq (25/28+o(1))N $, where $25/28 \approx 0.892857$. Thus the constant is improved from approximately $0.892857$ to $0.827543$. The result does not resolve the conjectural $1/2$ asymptotic.
Computationally, the bound can be pushed even further, I’ve managed to get it to 319/390 (and it can almost certainly be pushed further). Though the path to 1/2 appears very difficult.

As the proof method that gives 25/28 can be generalized quite easily, it's mostly a matter of computation to see what you can optimally reach with this strategy. Quanyu Tang and I have looked into this and can probably get it below 0.8. Our paper on this has been half-finished for a while now, but we've both been busy with other projects recently, so haven't actually finished it yet. Also, Wangs preprint is AI-generated without any disclaimers to this effect.

Yes, I’ve been looking at this and computationally you can get quite far (though it may take time). The issue is getting to 1/2 - that’s where the difficulty lies and simple computation won’t get there. An approach I’ve had is fixing a scale t, and working with the top layer. The issue is I’ve found a range where this approach admits counterexamples
Wang's $667/806$ configuration uses divisors of $720=2^4\cdot3^2\cdot5$. So a natural next finite experiment is to add the prime $7$.
FWIW, I believe the problem is meant to be no solutions to $\frac{1}{a}=\frac{1}{b_1}+\dots+\frac{1}{b_k}$, i.e. with equality. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
